evals.prompt: I need to classify customer messages into categories: billing, technical, account, general. Should I use an inline signature or a class-based one?
evals.expected_output: A class-based dspy.Signature with a Literal type constraint on the output field, a docstring instruction, and dspy.InputField/dspy.OutputField. Recommend class-based because of the Literal type constraint.

assertions.name: uses_literal_types
assertions.description: Uses Literal[...] for the category output field, not a free-text str

assertions.name: uses_class_based_signature
assertions.description: Shows a class MySignature(dspy.Signature) definition, not just an inline string

assertions.name: includes_docstring
assertions.description: Signature class has a docstring that acts as the task instruction

evals.prompt: I want to extract structured invoice data from raw text — vendor, date, line items with quantities and prices. How do I define the DSPy signature?
evals.expected_output: A Pydantic BaseModel for LineItem and Invoice/InvoiceData, then a dspy.Signature with the Pydantic model as the output type using dspy.OutputField.

assertions.name: uses_pydantic_model
assertions.description: Defines one or more Pydantic BaseModel classes for the structured output

assertions.name: uses_nested_model
assertions.description: Uses a nested model (LineItem inside Invoice) since line items are a list

assertions.name: output_typed_with_pydantic
assertions.description: The dspy.Signature output field uses the Pydantic model as the type annotation

assertions.name: provider_agnostic
assertions.description: LM config uses generic provider (e.g. openai/gpt-4o-mini) with a comment showing alternatives

evals.prompt: My categories come from a database at runtime. How do I use a Literal type with a list I build dynamically?
evals.expected_output: Use Literal[tuple(categories_list)] not Literal[categories_list] — the tuple() wrapping is required for dynamic Literal construction.

assertions.name: uses_tuple_wrapping
assertions.description: Shows Literal[tuple(list)] not Literal[list] for dynamic categories

assertions.name: explains_why
assertions.description: Explains that Literal[list] silently fails at runtime — Literal requires a tuple, not a list, for dynamic values
